meta.assumptions_note: Market-cap weighting; Newey-West t-stats; rolling 6-month (126d) beta for kill-criteria. See docs/methodology.md. Event studies for the two event-driven theses are not yet implemented.

baskets.name: AI Power Demand
baskets.thesis: Structural datacenter load growth (AI training + inference) re-rates the independent power producers, merchant nuclear operators, and grid-equipment suppliers that can actually sell incremental electrons into a supply-constrained market. The claim is that this cohort DECOUPLES from the regulated-utility multiple: their earnings become a call option on load growth priced on power demand and PPA economics, not on an allowed regulated return on rate base.
baskets.falsifiable_prediction: Over the tracking horizon, the basket earns positive, statistically distinguishable alpha (t-stat > ~2) to a regulated-utility factor (XLU) after controlling for the broad market (SPY). I.e. the basket is not merely levered XLU.
baskets.kill_criterion: If, on a rolling 6-month window, basket beta to XLU is indistinguishable from 1 (95% CI contains 1.0) AND the XLU-alpha intercept is indistinguishable from 0, then "AI power is structurally different" is falsified — these are just utilities and the correct exposure is the cheaper, more liquid XLU.

verdict.headline: XLU β=0.83, α=+37.1%/yr
verdict.interpretation: UNDERPOWERED, not dead. The utilities-alpha point estimate is large and positive (+37%/yr) but not yet significant (t=1.6), and the rolling XLU-beta CI is wide. With only ~2 years since GEV's 2024 spin-off and ~48% annualized vol, the test cannot yet reject either the thesis or its kill-criterion. Crucially the kill-criterion requires alpha ≈ 0, and a +37%/yr point estimate is NOT that — so calling the thesis dead here would be a low-power error. Verdict stays open pending more history.

baskets.name: Autonomy / Defense
baskets.thesis: A structural shift in defense procurement toward software-defined systems and attritable (low-cost, expendable) autonomous hardware re-rates a cohort of non-prime contractors. The claim has two testable parts: (1) it rides a budget regime change rather than the equity-market cycle, so it should show LOW market beta and behave as a diversifier; and (2) it responds measurably to defense-budget and geopolitical catalysts.
baskets.falsifiable_prediction: Basket market beta (to SPY) is materially below 1, the basket earns positive alpha to a defense-sector factor (ITA/XAR), and it shows positive abnormal return around defense-budget / major geopolitical events (event study, Phase 3).
baskets.kill_criterion: If market beta is >= ~1.2 with no measurable budget-event sensitivity (event-window abnormal return ~ 0, no alpha to ITA/XAR), then this is just high-beta tech wearing a defense label — the "diversifier / budget-regime" claim is falsified.

verdict.headline: market β=1.28 (budget-event test pending)
verdict.interpretation: Market beta is high (≥1.2), which leans toward the kill-criterion ('just high-beta tech') — but the budget-event test is still needed to fully adjudicate the 'diversifier' claim.
untestable_now: Budget-event / geopolitical-catalyst sensitivity requires a dated defense-budget event catalog (event study) — not yet implemented.

baskets.name: Onshoring Semis
baskets.thesis: Export controls plus capex reshoring shift value toward the "picks and shovels" of the semiconductor supply chain — the fab-equipment makers and the leading-edge foundry — and away from fabless designers whose end demand is more cyclical and more exposed to policy-driven market access. Owning the toll road (litho + WFE + foundry capacity) should out-earn owning any single chip design through a capex super-cycle.
baskets.falsifiable_prediction: The basket shows positive event-driven excess return, beyond its SOXX beta, in the [-1, +3] trading-day window around capex-guide and export-policy announcements (event study, Phase 3). Absent events, it need not beat SOXX.
baskets.kill_criterion: If daily returns are fully explained by SOXX beta (R^2 high, event-window abnormal return indistinguishable from 0 across the catalog of policy/capex events), there is no onshoring-specific edge — the basket is a more expensive way to hold SOXX.

verdict.headline: SOXX β=0.77, R²=0.68 (event study pending)
verdict.interpretation: Full-sample returns look largely explained by SOXX beta (R²=0.68, alpha not significant), which leans toward the kill-criterion — but the event study is still required to confirm there is no event-specific edge.
untestable_now: Event-driven alpha around capex-guide / export-policy announcements requires a hand-curated dated event catalog (event study) — not yet implemented, so the 'edge beyond SOXX beta' claim cannot be adjudicated.

baskets.name: Quantum
baskets.thesis: Pure-play quantum-computing equities are pre-revenue optionality: prices are set by milestone/narrative catalysts (qubit counts, error-correction demos, government awards) and by long-duration discount-rate sensitivity, not by current fundamentals. The honest claim is not "this outperforms" — it is "this behaves like a lottery ticket on a long-dated real option, and its risk signature (very high vol, rate sensitivity, jump-on-milestone) is distinguishable from a generic growth basket."
baskets.falsifiable_prediction: The basket exhibits (a) realized volatility well above ARKK, (b) a positive, significant loading on long-duration rates (TLT), and (c) discrete milestone-event jumps in the return series.
baskets.kill_criterion: If the basket's return signature is statistically indistinguishable from ARKK (beta ~ 1 to ARKK, no incremental TLT loading, no idiosyncratic milestone jumps), then it carries no information beyond "long speculative growth" and should be labeled as such — i.e. it is just ARKK with extra steps.

verdict.headline: ARKK β=0.97, TLT t=0.3
verdict.interpretation: The sleeve is statistically DISTINCT from ARKK, but via VOLATILITY, not rate sensitivity: realized vol is 2.4x ARKK and the rolling ARKK-beta CI sits well above 1.0. So it is not merely 'ARKK with extra steps'. The rate-sensitivity leg of the prediction, however, did NOT hold: the TLT loading is insignificant (t=0.3). This is NOT a claim of outperformance — the sleeve stays labeled as a lottery ticket on a long-dated real option, exactly as intended.
